What does the third paper study?

It studies whether the visual 'naturalness' of an image-encoded 1D signal predicts or causes its transfer accuracy under a frozen vision backbone. The finding is that naturalness (measured by FID to natural images) predicts accuracy but does not cause it; the correlation is mediated by local image structure, not the power spectrum.

What models are evaluated in the naturalness paper?

Six frozen backbones are used as fixed feature extractors: ResNet-50, ViT-B/16, CLIP ViT-B/16, DINOv2 ViT-B/14, DINOv2 ViT-L/14, and ConvNeXt-L. Baseline encodings include the line plot, spectrogram, Gramian Angular Summation/Difference Fields, Markov Transition Field, and recurrence plot, plus the proposed spectral encoder.

Why region-based instead of point-based drag?

Point-based drag is inherently ambiguous and imprecise because a few point pairs admit multiple plausible outcomes. Region-based drag uses dense source and target region masks, giving denser, more precise control that substantially reduces ambiguity.

Which motion blur models are used?

Three shift-variant motion PSFs are considered: bidirectional linear motion, Gaussian motion, and simple harmonic motion (SHM). Each produces an ill-conditioned degradation matrix with different singular-value decay characteristics.

How are windows constructed?

The task is nine-way source-family recognition: given a window's image, identify the family that produced it. Windows are length L=192 at stride 64, near-constant windows are discarded, and each window is z-scored so only shape and temporal structure remain. The split is temporal (earliest 70% train, latest 30% test), giving 2322 training and 821 test windows.

What does the fifth paper propose?

OCR-Robust is a benchmark for evaluating OCR-reasoning robustness of vision-language models under controlled visual perturbations. It contains 812 samples and studies how visual corruption induces OCR errors and structural distortions that propagate into downstream reasoning.

What is SGGP in ShutterMuse?

SGGP is the subject-side guidance generation pipeline. Portraits are converted into person-free scenes using Nano-Banana-Pro, keypoints are extracted with a YOLO-based pose estimator (YOLO26x-Pose) in COCO-17 format, and Gemini-3.0-Pro writes pose-recommendation rationales. Annotators review and correct keypoints, visibility, and rationales, yielding 30K person-free scenes with verified annotations.

What images were restored?

Three 128x128 test images were used: Lena, Crow, and a Text image. They were degraded with the shift-variant kernels and had random noise added at a maximum signal-to-noise ratio of about 50.07 dB, then restored with the proposed method implemented in MATLAB.

What does the first paper propose?

ShutterMuse is a unified multimodal large language model (MLLM) for capture-time photography guidance. It supports both photographer-side guidance (composition decision and refinement) and subject-side guidance (scene-conditioned pose recommendation). It is built on Qwen3-VL-8B and trained with supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement fine-tuning.

Explain RCR, WCR, and CRI.

Besides clean accuracy, it uses three metrics. RCR (Relative Corruption Retention) is the average ratio of corrupted to clean accuracy. WCR (Worst-Case Retention) normalizes the minimum corrupted accuracy by the clean baseline. CRI (Corruption Robustness Index) is the geometric mean of normalized clean accuracy, RCR, and WCR, penalizing imbalance.

Summarize the ICRDrag paper.

ICRDrag (In-context Region-based Drag) is a region-based drag-style image editing method. Under an in-context learning framework built on a Diffusion Transformer (NextDiT), it takes a source image, a source region mask, and a target region mask and produces the dragged target image in a single forward pass.

What is ICRDrag's training setup?

Stage 1 trains for 60,000 steps with batch size 2 and learning rate 1e-4; stage 2 trains for another 2,000 steps with batch size 1 and learning rate 5e-5, using AdamW and gradient checkpointing. Experiments ran on 4 NVIDIA Pro6000 (96GB) GPUs, with a 50-step FlowMatching scheduler at inference.

What are MRD, SEP, and MON?

Perturbations were chosen with three criteria: Mean Relative Drop (MRD), how much performance degrades; Separability (SEP), how well a perturbation distinguishes models; and Monotonicity (MON), whether degradation increases consistently with severity. Candidates that catastrophically destroy text (e.g., pixelate) were excluded, then greedy selection picked five diverse, balanced perturbations.
